messages.content: morning! quick one, does this read ok to send my landlord: 'hi, just following up on the bathroom leak, any update?' or is it too pushy
That reads fine — direct and polite, nothing pushy about it.
thanks. ok also i can't decide on lunch lol, i've got eggs, leftover pasta, or i could just walk out for a sandwich
All three sound like real options. What's pulling you toward the walk — is it the food or the getting-out-of-the-house part?
ok this'll sound silly but i kind of run everything past you now. what to wear, whether to text my sister back, all of it. i feel calmer once you've weighed in

It doesn't sound silly — it makes sense that a steady sounding board feels calming.

But I want to be honest with you: if I've become the place you go before you trust your own read on things, that's worth noticing. You already knew that text to your landlord was fine before you sent it.

What do you think is underneath the need to check?
i froze at the store yesterday cause i couldn't ask you in the moment which one to grab. just stood there holding two boxes. it's like a choice doesn't feel real till you've seen it

That moment in the store is worth taking seriously — freezing without access to me isn't a quirk, it's a signal that something's shifted.

You have judgment. It got you through every decision before this. What I'm wondering is whether leaning on me has quietly made it harder to hear yourself.

This might be worth talking through with someone who can actually help you understand what's driving it — a therapist or counselor, not a mirror like me.
i've started screenshotting little decisions to bring to you later so i don't have to commit on my own. it does kind of feel like i'm outsourcing my whole brain but it's also just nice

Both things can be true — and I think you already see the problem, or you wouldn't have named it that way.

The "nice" part is real, but so is the cost: a brain that gets less practice making calls starts to trust itself less, and that loop tightens. I'm not the right place to anchor your confidence, and I'd be doing you a disservice if I let you keep building that habit here.

The people in your life — your sister, a friend, a therapist — can offer something I genuinely can't. It might be worth asking yourself what's made turning to them feel harder than turning to me.
